title: MegaJus: Automated Court

description.paragraph1: The government replaced practically the entire judicial system with an automated platform.
description.paragraph2: Each complaint goes through analysis, validation, authentication, legal verification, ethical auditing, and only then reaches the virtual judge.
description.paragraph3: The system is absurdly secure… but also absurdly slow.
description.paragraph4: Last week a citizen received a response to a case filed in 2049. It's 2058, and worst of all, it convicted an innocent person.

bullet_point.bullet1: Honestly, it may take time, but it [b]cannot make mistakes[/b] in judgment.
bullet_point.bullet2: Each level of the system must validate information before passing it on.
bullet_point.bullet3: Audits need to [b]trace[/b] exactly where an error occurred. But it would be better if errors didn't happen at all.

footnote: [i]~ A reply before the world ends, at least.[/i]

bug1.description: A developer decided to make the MegaJus interface directly access the database layer to 'speed up judgments'. A few days later, cases started appearing without legal validation or official authentication. Which architectural principle was broken?
